text: The surgeon performs a circumferential rectotomy using a monopolar hook, executing a transanal proctectomy through a single-port transanal device. The rectum is mobilized along the bad TME plane to preserve pelvic innervation. Concurrently, a second surgical team identifies and sections the inferior mesenteric vessel, mobilizing the rectum anteriorly through the stoma site using a single-port laparoscopic device. Both teams continue dissection until they meet, and the proctectomy specimen is extracted through the stoma site.

The surgeon performs elongation maneuvers, including a mesenteric transverse incision and bowstring removal. The iliocolic vessels are ligated to maximize reach. Indocyanine green is injected intravenously, and fluorescence angiography is conducted, revealing optimal tissue perfusion.

The surgeon constructs the pouch using two fires of a 100mm linear stapler, placing the anvil of a 31mm circular stapler at the pouch apex. The pouch is approximated to the pelvis, and a double purse string anastomosis is created using the circular stapler. The integrity of the donuts is checked, followed by a transanal fluorescence angiography to assess the anastomosis, revealing a sectorial perfusion drop along the ileal mucosa at the left quadrant.

The surgeon takes down the anastomosis and retrieves the pouch transdomally due to suspected ischemia. The pouch is excised along the proximal side of the circular staple line to spare the remnant rectal cuff. ICG angiography reveals ischemia at the efferent limb of the pouch, leading to transection of the ischemic segment. A new pouch is constructed after assessing perfusion. A purse string suture is placed at the remnant distal rectal cuff transanally, and the anvil is pulled through the cuff with the suture tied around it before creating the new anastomosis.

The surgeon evaluates the anastomosis transanally using fluorescence angiography, confirming optimal perfusion. A reverse air leak test is performed, showing no leaks. A protective ileostomy is created following the intraoperative adverse event.
